## [00:00 - 03:02]

There are two primary mistakes advertisers make related to ads optimization. Let's talk about those today. I wrote a blog post on that too. Go ahead, check it out at johnloomer.com slash optimization. So first of all, a little definition. When we're talking about ads optimization, I'm referring to ad set optimization for delivery. How is it that meta is delivering your ads, optimizing, making changes, what is it based on? So there are two primary mistakes I see over and over that lead to frustration and wasted budget. So the first confusing objective with ad set optimization, the objective itself and the campaign is super important. It determines what options are going to be available to you within the ad set. But beyond that, far too often advertisers make the incorrect assumption that their objective by itself determines how their ads are delivered. So let's walk through an example. You may have selected the sales objective, but you go to the ad set conversion location website. What matters most is your performance goal. That's determining how your ads are delivered. That's what's going to matter way more than the objective within the performance goal options. Yes, you have maximized number of conversions, maximized value of conversions, maximized number of landing page views, link clicks, daily unique reach and impressions. Understand number of conversions, that doesn't necessarily mean purchases. You could select any of your conversion events. Once that's been selected, those could be value based or not. Meta doesn't prevent you from selecting other types of conversion events. So as we have here, I've selected search. That doesn't necessarily mean a purchase and it probably doesn't. So the first thing is yes, you have to select purchase for that to actually mean meta will deliver your ads with the focus of purchases. Beyond that, you can select landing page views, link clicks, daily unique reach and impressions. And some seem to think that if you use the sales objective, you could go with the landing page views performance goal and meta will deliver your ads with the thought that it's going to show to people who are likely to click on your ad and lead to a purchase. That's just not the case. There is zero mention of purchase or even conversions of any kind within these four performance goals. And even if you were to go into the traffic objective and go to your performance goal and you see all of these again, they're exactly the same. That's not a mistake. Your ads will be delivered in the exact same way. There's no evidence to the contrary. So the objective does not equal ad set optimization. Number two, optimizing for the wrong action. So let me give you an example. It may be expensive and difficult to optimize for a purchase, especially for low budget advertisers. So an advertiser may say, well, look about 5% of our traffic that lands on this product

## [03:02 - 05:06]

landing page converts. I know I can drive traffic to that landing page at 20 cents a pop if I optimize for a landing page view. If I can maintain that 5% conversion rate and 20 cents per landing page view, I'm going to be able to spend 20 bucks to get five purchases. If that's right, I think that's right. That is a crazy assumption, but I get why people think it. They assume the traffic sent from ads is the same as any other traffic on your website. But where this goes wrong is that meta ads optimization is literal. So the only focus, if you optimize for link clicks, landing page views, post engagement, whatever it is, the only focus is getting you as many of that thing as possible within your budget. It doesn't matter if they're low quality, if these are people who click on everything for whatever reason, if they're accidental clicks or click fraud and bots before they're detected, all the meta knows is you've said, I want landing page views. The algorithm doesn't care if no one purchases and it assumes you don't either. So the bottom line for this item, this big mistake is optimized for the action that you want. Even if you have a low budget, if you want purchases, optimize for purchases. At least prioritize that at first. I know that you want to exit the learning phase if possible, even if you can't. By using purchases as your conversion event and conversions as your performance goal, you're at least aligned with meta and the algorithm in terms of what defined success and how your ads are delivered. If you try to get cute, if you try to outsmart the algorithm by optimizing for something crazy like link clicks, landing page view, post engagement, you're only going to be disappointed, frustrated, and probably throw a whole bunch of money away. All right, so those are the two primary mistakes that advertisers make related to ad set optimization. Maybe there are more. Again, check out my blog post at joblumer.com slash optimization. These are two I see all the time, but certainly let me know if there are any that you think of.
